UserProfile.title: UserProfile
UserProfile.description: Represents the user's local profile and application settings within MediMind. Authentication data is assumed to be handled by an external system.

id.description: Unique identifier for the UserProfile entity.

name.description: The user's display name or nickname.

language.description: The preferred language for the application user interface (e.g., 'en' for English, 'ar' for Arabic).

themePreference.description: The user's preference for the app's visual theme ('light' or 'dark').

biometricLockEnabled.description: Indicates whether biometric authentication (fingerprint/face ID) is enabled to protect access to the app.

defaultNotificationSound.description: The identifier or path to the default sound file to be used for notifications, if set by the user.

Medicine.title: Medicine
Medicine.description: Represents a specific medication that the user is managing, including its dosage, status, and inventory tracking details.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Medicine entity.

userId.description: Reference to the UserProfile entity who owns this medicine. (Relationship: UserProfile 1:N Medicine)

name.description: The commercial or generic name of the medicine.

dosageAmount.description: The numerical amount of the medicine to be taken per dose.

dosageUnit.description: The unit of measurement for the dosage (e.g., 'mg', 'ml', 'pill', 'spray', 'capsule').

notes.description: Optional free-text notes or instructions related to the medicine or its usage.

isActive.description: Indicates whether the medicine is currently active and its doses should be scheduled and tracked.

currentQuantity.description: The current remaining quantity of the medicine in the user's inventory. Automatically decreases when a dose is marked as taken.

refillThreshold.description: The quantity level at which the user should be alerted that the medicine is running low and needs a refill.

Schedule.title: Schedule
Schedule.description: Defines a specific recurring pattern or set of times for a medicine to be taken, allowing for complex scheduling.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Schedule entity.

medicineId.description: Reference to the Medicine entity this schedule belongs to. (Relationship: Medicine 1:N Schedule)

timeOfDay.description: An array of specific times of day (e.g., '10:00', '22:30') for doses. Used for 'daily' and 'specificWeekdays' schedule types, allowing multiple dose times per day per schedule rule.

intervalHours.description: The number of hours between doses, specifically used for the 'everyXHours' schedule type.

weekdays.description: An array of specific weekdays (e.g., ['Monday', 'Wednesday', 'Friday']) when doses should be scheduled. Used for 'specificWeekdays' type.

startDate.description: The date from which this schedule becomes active.

endDate.description: The optional end date when this schedule ceases to be active. If null, the schedule continues indefinitely.

notificationChannelId.description: The identifier for the notification channel to use for doses generated by this schedule, allowing for customized notification behavior.

notificationSound.description: The sound file or identifier for notifications for doses from this schedule, overriding the user's default setting.

DoseLog.title: DoseLog
DoseLog.description: Records the history of a single scheduled dose, including its original schedule, actual status, and timing, for adherence tracking and analytics.

id.description: Unique identifier for the DoseLog entity.

medicineId.description: Reference to the Medicine entity related to this dose. (Relationship: Medicine 1:N DoseLog)

scheduleId.description: Reference to the Schedule entity that generated this specific dose instance. (Relationship: Schedule 1:N DoseLog)

scheduledTime.description: The exact date and time the dose was originally scheduled to be taken.

actualTime.description: The exact date and time the dose was actually taken, skipped, or when a 'remind me later' action was performed.

status.description: The current status of the dose ('scheduled', 'taken', 'skipped', 'missed', 'reminded_later'). 'Missed' implies it passed scheduled time without action.

notes.description: Optional notes related to this specific dose event (e.g., reason for skipping, side effects).

systemNotificationId.description: The ID assigned by the local notification system (e.g., Flutter local notifications plugin) for the notification associated with this dose. Useful for cancellation or updates.

definition.description: Stores individual user profiles and application settings. The document ID `{userId}` corresponds to the authenticated user's UID. Ownership is implicitly tied to the path and the 'id' field within the document.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique ID of the authenticated user (Firebase Authentication UID).

definition.description: Contains details for each medicine a user is tracking. Includes denormalized 'userId' field for authorization independence, matching the parent path's user ID.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique ID of the authenticated user who owns this medicine.

params.name: medicineId
params.description: The unique ID of the medicine document.

definition.description: Defines the recurring patterns and times for taking a specific medicine. Includes denormalized 'userId' for authorization independence (must be added to the entity schema in practice).

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique ID of the authenticated user who owns the parent medicine and this schedule.

params.name: medicineId
params.description: The unique ID of the parent medicine.

params.name: scheduleId
params.description: The unique ID of the schedule document.

definition.description: Records the history and status of individual scheduled doses. Includes denormalized 'userId' for authorization independence (must be added to the entity schema in practice).

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique ID of the authenticated user who owns the parent medicine and this dose log.

params.name: medicineId
params.description: The unique ID of the parent medicine.

params.name: doseLogId
params.description: The unique ID of the dose log entry.

firestore.reasoning:
The Firestore structure for MediMind prioritizes secure, scalable, and debuggable authorization by strictly following Authorization Independence and Structural Segregation principles. All user-specific data is nested under `/users/{userId}`, creating a private data silo for each user. This path-based ownership (`/users/{userId}/...`) ensures that all data within a user's hierarchy is inherently owned by and accessible only to that specific user (`request.auth.uid == userId`).

**Authorization Independence (CRITICAL):**
To guarantee authorization independence and avoid inefficient and insecure `get()` calls in security rules, the `userId` field is explicitly denormalized into every subcollection document that logically belongs to a user. Specifically:

*   **UserProfile:** Located at `/users/{userId}`, its `id` field matches the `userId` wildcard, and implicitly `request.auth.uid`. No further denormalization needed.
*   **Medicine:** Located at `/users/{userId}/medicines/{medicineId}`, its schema already includes `userId`, aligning perfectly with the path and `request.auth.uid` for direct ownership verification (`resource.data.userId == request.auth.uid`).
*   **Schedule:** Located at `/users/{userId}/medicines/{medicineId}/schedules/{scheduleId}`. Although nested, for strict Authorization Independence, the `userId` of the owning user MUST be denormalized and stored within each `schedule` document. This allows security rules to check `resource.data.userId == request.auth.uid` directly on the `schedule` document without performing a `get()` operation on its parent `medicine` or `user` document.
*   **DoseLog:** Located at `/users/{userId}/medicines/{medicineId}/doseLogs/{doseLogId}`. Similarly, the `userId` of the owning user MUST be denormalized and stored within each `doseLog` document. This enables direct ownership verification `resource.data.userId == request.auth.uid` on the `doseLog` document, preventing reliance on parent document lookups.

This denormalization ensures that any security rule applied to `Schedule` or `DoseLog` documents can verify ownership solely based on the document's own data, enabling atomic writes and reads, simplifying rule logic, and improving performance.

**QAPs (Query Access Patterns) Support:**
This structure inherently supports secure and efficient Query Access Patterns (QAPs) through the use of strong hierarchical paths and structural segregation:

*   **User Profile Access:** `get('/users/' + request.auth.uid)` directly accesses the user's profile. No `list` operations are expected for user profiles by other users.
*   **Medicine Listing:** `list` operations on `/users/{userId}/medicines` are secure because the `{userId}` wildcard in the path must match `request.auth.uid`. This naturally filters results to only the current user's medicines.
*   **Schedule Listing:** `list` operations on `/users/{userId}/medicines/{medicineId}/schedules` are secure, as they are implicitly filtered by `userId` (matching `request.auth.uid`) and `medicineId`. The denormalized `userId` within each `schedule` document reinforces this.
*   **DoseLog Listing/Analytics:** `list` operations on `/users/{userId}/medicines/{medicineId}/doseLogs` allow for fetching dose history for a specific medicine, securely filtered by `userId` and `medicineId`. Further queries for analytics across all medicines for a user (e.g., all dose logs for `request.auth.uid` for a given time range) could be facilitated by querying the `doseLogs` collection group, which would still be secured by `resource.data.userId == request.auth.uid` on each document.

**Structural Segregation:** Each collection (`users`, `medicines`, `schedules`, `doseLogs`) has a homogeneous security posture: all documents within these collections are owned by and exclusively accessible to the user identified by the `{userId}` in their path (and by the `userId` field denormalized within the document data). There's no mixing of public/private or different roles within the same collection, simplifying rule writing immensely. This design creates clear boundaries and makes the application's data access highly predictable and manageable.
